item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image could not be sourced, assumed non-compliant, and the second image comes from here with no indication the original photographer has been contacted. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sinus infection? Sadly no. You have nasal crabs.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It can "control" people by messing up their vision, basically forcing them to go exactly where the crabs want by just messing with the optical nerves, suggesting some degree of sapience. It can also live in lava… Anomalous enough for me.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really cannot see what's anomalous about this. This doesn't feel like some bizarre anomaly to be contained and obscured by the SCPF, but to be widely publicized and studied under the coordination of the WHO to find a cure or other means to combat it. Keeping something like this hidden when it can be explained by conventional science just makes it more difficult to inform people of the proper precautions against it, thus going against the Foundations mission to PROTECT.

I think it could work pretty well as an EX article though, if you include a some text showing what they thought was anomalous about it and then provide the scientific explanation.

As it is now, however, it essentially looks to me as a crustacean version of Ascaris lumbricoides (a parasitic roundworm), plus some lobotomy, so nothing we don't already have to deal with. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, as others have noted, not seeing what is anomalous about this. There are real-world parasites that are just as weird, if not moreso. There is literally nothing in the article that sets this particular creature outside the realm of science; only some vague hinting at something about their subterranean phase, which isn't sufficient to avoid a downvote.

Creepy does not a SCiP make. You want creepy, check out the Cordyceps spp. fungus, Hymenoepimecis argyraphaga wasps, Euhaplorchis californiensis trematode worms, Cymothoa exigua tongue-eating isopods, or the ever-popular Toxoplasma gondii. All very real and very creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forget headcrabs! THIS is terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like skips that don't violate known science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the main reason this is an SCP is the way it is dangerous to humans (and animals), and how it infects them. The way these creatures infect people are unusual and effective, and not to mention they can survive in the lava tubes, suggesting that they can or once could survive extreme heat.

These things can't be fully explained or discovered as of now, until the Foundation explores it's subterranean life cycle.

I myself really enjoyed this article, it was very interesting as well as creepy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ladies and gentlemen, one of the most dafuq SCPs.

I mean, crabs in you nose! Does that seem a bit dafuq to anyone else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Paraphrasing the Author Stereotypes guide, when in comes to Body Horror, Mother Nature got there first, and she's been doing it a lot longer then we have.

do we have an SCP for that yet?

Now I want a parody of the whole 'there's an app for that' in SCP form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow I loved this, but I can see where you take issue with it.

Really the only thing I can think of that might add that extra level of SCP-designated danger is by potentially hinting that these things turn into that ancient race of crab-people living under our cities plotting their ascension to retake the surface world from us air breathing monkeys (do we have an SCP for that yet?)

Personally though, I like the biological feasibility of it, and the medical squick was beautiful, so I'm up-voting it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with GuesssWho; realistic SCPs are great, but when they're too realistic, they're no longer SCP-worthy, regardless of how well written they are.

Basically the whole point of the Foundation is to contain "anomalies", as in stuff what can't be explained or very clearly isn't terrestrial. A bat that can travel through more than 3 dimensions, a spooky ghosty thing, a living couch that acts like a big dog. Basically 9 parts realism, 1 part "Magic McGuffin"/"Unobtanium" secret ingredient that shouldn't (or doesn't) warrant explanation in order to add mystery and/or intrigue.

Meanwhile, there are species of parasite and insect and so on that are basically exactly like this SCP, only targetting other animals.

Discovering a Spider Crab like this wouldn't be something to warrant "TAKE IT, HIDE IT, COVER IT UP, BURN IT ALL, KEEP IT HIDDEN AT ALL COSTS" like an SCP-140 or SCP-682. Discovering this would be a breakthrough in science and biology—a stunning new species of spider. Just because it's dangerous to humans doesn't mean it should be suppressed and exterminated —plenty of other animals are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is being "far to realistic" worthy of a downvote!?

Making an SCP realistic makes it more horrifying because one that could actually exist could, by definition, feasibly exist in the real world, thus, making it more scary!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nasty, but far too realistic. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it all depends on how far you're willing to dig. If there's not enough straightforward information to indicate the impossibility of the SCP according to modern science, that doesn't necessarily mean the SCP is possible by modern science. There's still a substantial amount of information that's left for the reader to ponder. For example:

SCP-1104's subterranean lifecycle has not been documented.

We don't know what these things do once they leave human hosts, we don't know how they breed, we don't know why Site-104 lava tubes irregularly spit these things up by the hundreds. We don't know where they come from, I don't think there's any biological or evolutionary precedence that would explain them, and we don't know how they came into existence. There's plenty of existing documentation on the evolution of cordyceps, but for this SCP, it's up to the reader to decide whether or not to rule out scientific probability.

Might be easily mistaken for a booger, but I don't see the horror in that. Is that it, by the way? Is that what the fuss is about? That it could easily be mistaken for something that happens all the time?

Well, if dead versions of these can be mistaken for boogers, which everyone encounters, then the spread of the crabs is pretty darn wide, and for all we know there could be one in everyone's nose right now, since only one crab can be in your nose at a time (note the H1 receptor antagonists) so if there's a dead one in your nose, you were probably subjected to a source or traveling airborne population of nose crabs recently, so there may be some on you right now and you wouldn't notice (0.4mm is pretty darn small).

Granted, this is all conjecture on my part and the best person to ask these questions to would be the author, but that's my take on things. The article is rather subtle in the respects stated above, but I actually rubbed my nose in discomfort a few times while reading it.

Edit: Your questions are all valid, so there's no need to call yourself an SCP Nazi. :) It's kind of a weird thing to call yourself, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why? I don't get it. Yes, that would remove some of the horror if there was a way to get this thing out of the body without damaging the host. I'm thinking very hard right now about dried-up crabs in a person's nose, but no matter how hard I try, I can't figure out what merits the "God save us all!" response. It'd just fall out, right? Might be easily mistaken for a booger, but I don't see the horror in that. Is that it, by the way? Is that what the fuss is about? That it could easily be mistaken for something that happens all the time? That, on its own, isn't very horrifying as far as I can tell…I'm confused…or stupid…or both. If someone could please spell this out for me, I would be very grateful.

As for the scip itself, I must, of course, make the standard challenge: What makes these crabs impossible according to modern science? The fact that they're specific to humans? They're not, any mammal can be infected. Parasites are common, and most of them are highly disturbing; what makes these crabs so impossible that they have to be contained by the Foundation? I'd upvote because OH GOD LOBOTOMIZING PARASITE CRAB, but I have to downvote just because it's not really a scip. Remember, the SCP Foundation doesn't just contain things because they're disturbing, or even because they're potentially world-ending; they contain things because they cannot be explained by modern science - which generally causes them to be disturbing, fear of the unknown and all that, but "disturbing" is not the point of the Foundation, at least not in-universe.

Ladies and gentlemen, this has been the SCP Nazi, thank you very much, I'll probably be back next week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I already hated crabs before this.

No further comment.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you see where I'm going with this

No…? They die? Which is good? But that removes some of the horror, if you can kill them easily, they're…

oh God save us all

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two suggestions:

- If the host fails to take them to the source of gas for long enough, they will atrophy and shrink down to a tiny size (< 1cm) and eventually dries up. (I think you see where I'm going with this.)

- The foundation currently has several dead mature Phase 1 specimens, obtained by piping hydrogen sulfide into the room through a vent in the bottom of an open box. When the subject passes out and the crab emerges and jumps into the box to get at the hydrogen sulfide, the lid slams shut, trapping the creature. Proposals to set up a simulated volcanic environment with hidden cameras in an attempt to record the creature's full life cycle have thus far been denied, primarily due to budget constraints.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always had a problem with parasites. The fact that everyone gets general headaches makes it worse. This creeps the hell out of me. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't quite see Keter in this yet. Could they perhaps establish more nests? I think this is the kinda thing that could use an incident report to better show the threat is poses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I must be extra picky about zoological SCPs, because I love that kind of stuff and have seen it done so well in other works of fiction …. and something about either the description or the last stage of the lifecycle bugs me. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a crab whose larval form floats into your nose, crawls up into your sinus cavity, fucks with your eyes until you come to a source of awful smelling gas, and then knocks you out by nearly lobotomizing you and then CRAWLS OUT THROUGH YOUR EYE, or (possibly worse) AROUND IT.

GAH FUCK JESUS DAMN SHIT

…….. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And it'll need to be a colon if the author puts in the second phase. I realize now that's actually how I read it the first time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture used shows a crab that is clearly too large for the SCP. I find this to be distracting from the entry. Wikipedia's entry under 'crab' has several pictures of crabs with blank backgrounds, allowing them to be any size the author desires.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least fixed the fact that that semicolon should be a comma. The rest of it is not for me to assume.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spider crabs: almost as bad as actual spiders.

Well, that was a trip! I'd been thinking lately that no one could pull off a decent Keter anymore, but here we are! I upvoted, but I have one concern:

The lifecycle of SCP-1104 comprises at least two distinct phases; the first being a larval stage approximately 0.4mm in diameter.

There's no description of the second phase, and this feels like an oversight. How big do the adults get? I got a sense from reading the full description, but having it up front would be nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
concentrations up to 200 individuals per square meter.

I think you mean "per cubic meter".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely squick. However, I would recommend removing the 'tentatively named' bit. If these things are reasonably well documented and are to be killed on sight, being in one genus or another won't change protocol. Still an upvote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I thought, "these are creepy, but why are they keter?"

And then I read the last line and said, "oh, they're keter because we can't contain them and they lobotomize you even if you remove them surgically".

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nose crabs. *Shiver*

Upvoted.
